items.question: Who owns the encoding workflow versus the one-hot and target encoding definitions?
items.answer: Feature engineering encoding (this topic) owns pipeline composition, cardinality decisions, and per-column strategy selection. Data preprocessing Encoding Categoricals owns canonical definitions and leakage pitfalls for one-hot, ordinal, and target encoding.

items.question: What is categorical encoding in a feature-engineering pipeline?
items.answer: The engineering step that maps each categorical column to a model-ready numeric representation—chosen by semantics and cardinality—and fits encoders inside ColumnTransformer or Pipeline so train and serve use identical mappings.

items.question: When should you switch from one-hot to frequency or target encoding?
items.answer: When training cardinality is high enough that full one-hot expansion hurts memory, sparsity, or overfitting—often hundreds to thousands of levels—after checking model family and applying leakage-safe fitting for target statistics.

items.question: Where in the sklearn stack do categorical encoders belong?
items.answer: Inside ColumnTransformer branches, wrapped with the estimator in Pipeline, fit on training folds only, then serialized as one artifact for validation, test, and production transform calls.

items.question: Why serialize the full Pipeline instead of the model alone?
items.answer: The model weights assume a fixed encoded feature matrix—one-hot vocabularies, ordinal level orders, and frequency maps learned at train time. Serving the model without its encoders produces wrong column counts or mappings and broken predictions.

items.question: How do you decide encoding per column in practice?
items.answer: For each column: classify nominal vs ordinal, count training nunique, match to model capabilities (linear vs native categorical trees), pick one-hot, ordinal, bucketing, frequency, hash, or CV target encoding per Encoding Categoricals rules, and document unseen-category policy.

items.question: How does a ColumnTransformer workflow compare to manual pandas get_dummies in notebooks?
items.answer: ColumnTransformer plus Pipeline records encoder state at fit time and reapplies it with transform at serve time. Manual get_dummies in notebooks often omits the same step in production, causing column mismatches and non-reproducible feature matrices.

items.question: How does frequency encoding compare to one-hot for high-cardinality nominal columns?
items.answer: One-hot creates one sparse binary column per level—interpretable but expensive at thousands of levels. Frequency encoding maps each level to a single training-set count or proportion—compact and tree-friendly but loses per-level coefficients and needs a defined unseen fallback.

items.question: What breaks if you one-hot encode a 100,000-level user_id column for logistic regression?
items.answer: Feature space explodes to 100k sparse columns—RAM exhaustion, hours-long training, and severe overfitting as the model memorizes individual users instead of generalizable signals; evaluation on new users collapses.

items.question: What breaks if production calls fit_transform on incoming batches daily?
items.answer: Category vocabularies and dummy columns change over time while model weights stay fixed—predictions become inconsistent, feature alignment errors appear, and offline metrics no longer match live behavior.
